title: Acyclicity

tex_statement.html: A CDMG $G=(J,V,E,L)$ is called acyclic if there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk from $v$ to itself in $G$ for any node $v \in G$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- The CDMG `G` is *acyclic*: no node admits a non-trivial directed
walk to itself. Mirrors `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def
3.6 (`\label{def-acylic}`) verbatim, with "non-trivial" read as
`1 ≤ π.length` per `Walks.lean`'s `Walk.length` doc. The trivial walk
`Walk.nil v` is excluded by the `1 ≤ π.length` conjunct (it has
length zero), so a witness against acyclicity is genuinely a
directed cycle through `v` of one or more steps. Used as the
precondition for claim_3_2 (`AcyclicIffTopologicalOrder`), def_3_7
(graph type names), and pervasively in chapters 4 -- 16. -/
def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG α) : Prop :=
  ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ ∃ π : Walk G v v, π.IsDirected ∧ 1 ≤ π.length
lean_code_without_comments:
def IsAcyclic (G : CDMG α) : Prop :=
  ∀ v ∈ G, ¬ ∃ π : Walk G v v, π.IsDirected ∧ 1 ≤ π.length
lean_explanation:
The encoding sits one floor above the walk-data layer of def 3.4. The ambient binder `α : Type*` is the vertex carrier; `G : CDMG α` is the bundled graph from def 3.1 and is an explicit argument so that downstream callers can speak of acyclicity for distinct graphs (e.g. chapter 5's intervened graph $G^{do(W)}$). The quantifier `∀ v ∈ G, ...` uses the `Membership α (CDMG α)` instance from def 3.2: `v ∈ G` unfolds to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`, so the LN's literal "any node $v \in G$" lifts verbatim. The witness `π : Walk G v v` is the umbrella walk inductive of def 3.4 item 1 with both endpoints set to `v` — note that the trivial walk `Walk.nil v : Walk G v v` always exists at length zero, so the predicate has to exclude it explicitly rather than ban all self-walks.

Two predicates from `WalkPredicates.lean` carry the LN's adjectives. `Walk.IsDirected` (def 3.4 item 2) is a structural recursion asserting every step is a `forward` constructor, i.e. every edge points in the LN's $\tuh$ direction; it is vacuously true on `Walk.nil`. `Walk.length` (the LN's parameter $n$ in $v = v_0, \ldots, v_n = w$) is the number of `cons` constructors, so `1 ≤ π.length` is the LN's "non-trivial" condition — it rules out exactly the trivial walk, which is the only `Walk G v v` of length zero.

The shape of the encoding is therefore a single `Prop`-valued definition: for every node `v` of the graph, *no* walk from `v` to itself is simultaneously directed and of length at least one. The double-negation (`¬ ∃`) phrasing matches the LN's "there does not exist any non-trivial directed walk". Because the predicate sits in the `CDMG` namespace, downstream Lean reads as `G.IsAcyclic`, matching the LN's recurring prose "$G$ is acyclic" that is invoked across def 3.7 (graph type names), claim 3.2 (acyclic iff topological order), claim 3.8 (acyclicity preserved under intervention), and the entire CBN / SCM / discovery edifice of chapters 4–16.
design_choices:
**Acyclicity as a property of the graph, accessed via `G.IsAcyclic`.** The LN's prose treats acyclicity as a predicate *of* the graph — "the CDMG $G$ is acyclic". Placing the definition inside the `CDMG` namespace lets every downstream call read `G.IsAcyclic`, mirroring the LN literal. The natural alternative — a top-level `Causality.IsAcyclic G` — is slightly more compact but disconnects acyclicity from the family of CDMG-level operators (`Adjacent`, `EdgeInto`, `tuh`, `hut`, …) that share this namespace, and reads less like the LN's "$G$ is ..." prose at every later use site.

**Quantifier `∀ v ∈ G`, not `∀ v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`.** Both unfold to the same proposition via the `Membership` instance from def 3.2 — they are definitionally equal. The `v ∈ G` form is chosen because the LN literally writes "any node $v \in G$". The project-wide convention is to mirror LN prose in Lean binders; callers who need to peel `v ∈ G` back to `v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V` can do so with a single `simp` step.

**"Non-trivial" as `1 ≤ π.length`, not `π ≠ Walk.nil v` or `π.length ≠ 0`.** All three are propositionally equivalent at the endpoint match $v = v$, but `1 ≤ π.length` is the LN's own framing — def 3.4 parameterises walks by $n \ge 0$ and treats non-trivial as $n \ge 1$. The natural-number form composes cleanly with later length-based arguments (claim 3.2's induction on walk length; chapter 16's bifurcation bounds comparing `π.length` with `support.length`). The alternative `π ≠ Walk.nil v` would force an awkward case distinction at every use site and is not even equivalent in the general endpoint case — for `π : Walk G v w` with `v ≠ w`, length zero is structurally impossible, but for the diagonal case `Walk G v v` one must still discharge an endpoint-equality witness. The numeric form sidesteps that bookkeeping entirely.

**`Prop`, not `Type _`.** The LN uses acyclicity only as a precondition — "if $G$ is acyclic, then ..." — never as data carrying constructive content. A `Type _`-valued formulation would force every later "$G$ is acyclic" hypothesis to carry a witness around, cluttering signatures with no proof-theoretic gain (the predicate is decidable only in restricted settings, so the constructive content would not pay for itself). The `Prop` choice matches Mathlib's `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic` precedent and how the predicate is consumed downstream.

**Inline conjunction, no helper `IsNonTrivialDirectedCycle`.** The inner predicate `π.IsDirected ∧ 1 ≤ π.length` reads cleanly inline and appears in only this one place; introducing a named alias would add a layer of unfolding for downstream `simp`/rewrite steps without compensating readability. If a later row repeatedly references the same conjunction (claim 3.2 might want to name "directed cycle through $v$"), that row can introduce the helper locally.

**No `isAcyclic_iff` simp lemma.** The defining body is already the shape downstream callers want to reason against; an unfolding lemma would just be `Iff.rfl`. The useful rewrite — the contrapositive $\neg G.\text{IsAcyclic} \iff \exists v \in G, \exists \pi : \text{Walk}\ G\ v\ v, \pi.\text{IsDirected} \wedge 1 \le \pi.\text{length}$ via `push_neg` — is a one-line derivation that any caller can do; it is left for the row (most likely claim 3.2's `←` direction) that first needs it, rather than pre-emptively committing this file to a particular bundled-versus-unbundled existential shape.
